posts.text:
We are deeply saddened to confirm the death of one of our own, Spc. Sarah Beckstrom, a member of the West Virginia Army National Guard, who was shot in the line of duty near the Farragut Square Metro Station on November 26, 2025. Spc. Beckstrom was pronounced dead at MedStar Washington Hospital in the District of Columbia on November 27, 2025, as the result of wounds incurred during the shooting.

Spc. Beckstrom enlisted in the West Virginia National Guard on June 26, 2023, and served with distinction as a military police officer with the 863rd Military Police Company. She exemplified leadership, dedication, and professionalism.

Spc. Beckstrom, a 2023 graduate of Webster County High School, was a resident of Summersville, West Virginia. She volunteered to serve as part of Operation D.C. Safe and Beautiful helping to ensure the safety and security of our nation’s capital. Her loss is felt profoundly across our One Guard Family and throughout the Mountain State.

“On behalf of Governor Patrick Morrisey and the entire West Virginia National Guard, I extend my deepest condolences to Spc. Beckstrom’s family, friends, and fellow Guardsmen. We grieve alongside them and honor her memory by carrying forward her commitment to service, integrity, and excellence.” said Maj. Gen. Jim D. Seward, the Adjutant General of the West Virginia National Guard.

We ask that everyone respect the privacy of her family and her fellow Soldiers during this difficult time.

profile.occupation: The Adjutant General @ West Virginia National Guard | Soldier, Leadership, Policy Development [The postings on this site are my own and don’t represent the WVNG, NGB, the Army or DoW.]

comments.text:
Heavenly Father,

We come before You in urgent need. Lord, Your child has been wounded, and we ask for Your mighty hand to cover him right now. Surround him with Your angels, protect him from every danger, and let Your healing power flow through his body.

Father, touch every organ, every vein, every bone, every cell. Stop all bleeding, calm every fear, and guide the hands of every doctor, nurse, and responder caring for him. Bring strength where there is weakness, peace where there is panic, and life where there is threat.

Lord Jesus, You are the Great Physician. By Your stripes we are healed. We declare Your healing over him. We speak restoration, full recovery, and renewed strength. Let Your presence fill that room and drive out all pain, infection, or complications.

God, wrap his family in Your comfort. Replace their fear with faith, their worry with hope, and their shaking hearts with Your steady peace.

We trust in Your power, Your mercy, and Your love. Raise him up again, Lord. Let this be a testimony of Your protection and Your glory.

In Jesus’ mighty name we continue to pray.
Amen🙏

posts.text:
Hi everyone,

After a recent organizational restructuring, I’m taking this opportunity to explore what’s next—and I’m genuinely excited about the possibilities ahead. Over the years, I’ve had the privilege of leading teams, improving products and processes, and driving operational excellence. Seeing those efforts translate into winning cultures, safer workplaces, and improved customer experiences has been incredibly rewarding.

My background spans plant management, product management, and team leadership, and I’m passionate about solving complex problems and delivering results. Looking forward, my goal is to join an organization where I can lead transformative initiatives that enhance operational performance and support strategic growth, while continuing to develop innovative solutions that make a lasting impact.

If you know of any opportunities or can connect me with someone in your network, I’d greatly appreciate it. Please feel free to reach out directly or share this post—I’d love to start a conversation.

Thank you for your support!

#OpenToWork #OperationsLeadership #PlantManagement #ProductManagement #ContinuousImprovement #Leadership

posts.text:
Celebrating 5 Years at Microsoft 

If you had told me when I graduated from WVU in 2014 with a Bachelor of Arts in Criminology that I would one day be a Senior Software Engineer at Microsoft with a Master of Science in Software Engineering, I might not have believed you. Yet here I am in 2025, reflecting on an incredible journey filled with growth, opportunity, and gratitude.

First, I owe so much to GEICO’s Veterans IT Development Program, which gave me my start in programming and changed the trajectory of my career. A heartfelt thanks also to Deloitte, where I learned invaluable soft skills and the true power of an inclusive and collaborative organization.

I’m deeply grateful to the West Virginia Army National Guard for enabling me to earn my master’s degree debt-free through their exceptional tuition assistance programs. If you’re looking for the best deal in education, this is it!

My time as a commissioned Field Artillery Officer in the Army National Guard has taught me leadership and resilience in ways that no classroom or corporate training ever could.

To all the incredible civilian and military leaders and mentors I’ve had the privilege of learning from over the years—your guidance and support have truly shaped who I am today. Your impact on my life has been profound, and I am forever grateful.

This journey is a testament to the power of hard work and having clear goals. It truly is the American dream.

I’m incredibly thankful, humbled, and blessed to be where I am today. Here’s to the next chapter!

#Microsoft #SoftwareEngineering #ArmyNationalGuard #Gratitude #AmericanDream

posts.text:
I started my career in IT with a very similar program at GEICO (started by Ted Robey 🙌) I cannot applaud companies enough who host programs for veterans attempting to break into IT. For me this was literally a life changing program that skyrocketed me into a very successful and fruitful Software Engineering career. For my veteran colleagues, I cannot recommend this enough.

For my Microsoft colleagues I highly recommend signing up to be an MSSA mentor.

resharedPost.text:
I’ve seen the social media shouts for DoD to #fixourcomputers, so I did some basic benchmarking to illustrate my experience. I designed these tasks to represent everyday work activities that I could replicate on my Air Force laptop and personal computer. I performed each task three times, working as quickly as possible and recording how long it took me, and then selected the median times for comparison.

(The data may not be statistically significant, but it gets the point across. If the DoD wants a more *scientific* study, they can pay a contractor to write a whitepaper.)

My personal computer does have a better processor than my Air Force laptop, but I consider them comparable for simple tasks like checking email. (System details in a comment.) I also updated both systems before starting to avoid getting stuck in an update cycle.

On average, the Air Force computer was 5.4 times slower.

To shine a light on WHY the Air Force computer is so much slower, I performed another test:
  1. Restart each computer
  2. Log in
  3. Wait five minutes without doing anything
  4. Check the system resource usage

Here are those results:
- Air Force: 95% CPU Usage | 5.7 GB RAM Usage
- Personal: 1% CPU Usage | 3.1 GB RAM Usage

Five minutes after startup, my personal computer had reached a comfortable idle state while the Air Force CPU was still thrashing with McAfee, Tanium, and a bunch of other stuff.

The path to #fixourcomputers isn’t to simply buy newer, beefier computers (though that will certainly help). We’ve got to untangle the web of configuration scripts and software bloat dragging down our machines.

We can also continue shouting at the DoD as if it’s a dastardly villain, twirling its long mustache whilst intentionally slowing down our computers and laughing, “muahahahaha!” I doubt that will fix any problems… but it is kinda fun.

comments.text: Only so much can be said for centralization until it begins to strangle the system it was supposed to enhance

author.occupation: Project Manager, Satellite Services Inc.

comments.text:
This has been a frequent piece of my feedback on the HAF IT surveys.  Their computers seem to run in a bureaucratic fashion where each process, web request, and execution of an application has to go through multiple approvals before it can do it's job.  The systems I've been exposed to have always felt, and based on the specs, underpowered for the basic tasks that you measured against.  It almost feels like it has been designed to fail in order to justify a major shift in IT architecture.  Maybe in a few years we'll all be back to having a dumb terminal that connects to the cloud where the OS/system will be hosted.  
A good plan of attack would be to fix pieces of the problem.  Does the hardware procurement process and specs make sense?  How much management software and services are running on each box, is there unnecessary duplication?  How about the security side how are systems configured to scan, report, and update.  What about the network?  Are there bottlenecks due to firewalls, bandwidth restrictions, too many hops to get out?  Pick one of these and focus on fixing the problems then move on to the next one.  A 1% improvement each year it would be a welcome change from the continued degradation that seems to be going on.

comments.text:
First West Virginia!  

For those who don’t know that is the 1/201st Field Artillery Battalion. It was organized in 1735, and it is the oldest continually-active regiment in the U.S. Army. The 201st has been in engagements since the French and Indian War to the Iraqi War (of course participation in the American Revolution, Civil War both North and South, and both World Wars just to name a few).

As a prior member, I am extremely proud they are featured in this video representing the Army National Guard!

King of Battle!
